      Preface for the 40th Anniversary Edition
For this special 40th anniversary edition a new preface section has been added along with the inclusion of the classic scenario,
The Haunting, which can be found at the end of the book. These bonus pages do not affect the main book’s page numbering.



Sandy Petersen                                                            The book had a lengthy introduction (I assume by August
                                                                       Derleth, but I don’t actually recall) that talked about Lovecraft
In 1964, I was 9 years old. We lived in a rented house, and my         in some detail, plus praised other stories by him which were,
father’s books were kept in cardboard boxes in the basement.           frustratingly, not in this book—notably “The Thing on the
I was one of those annoying precocious kids who read a lot,            Doorstep,” whose title seemed promising. I was hooked.
so I would root through these boxes, looking for something             Lovecraft was now my favorite author.
interesting to check out. This is where I encountered both                A few months later, the book vanished. I couldn’t find it
Edgar Rice Burroughs and Edgar Allan Poe, among others.                anywhere. Three years later, when I was 12, a friend of mine
One day, while poring through these boxes, I found an ancient          returned the book. Apparently, I’d loaned it to him and hadn’t
paperback titled The Dunwich Horror and Other Stories by H.            remembered. Well, at least I had it back now.
P. Lovecraft. It was in an Armed Services edition, printed in             In 1969, I managed to find exactly one other Lovecraft
1942, and intended for distribution to American soldiers and           book, The Colour Out of Space and Others which contained
sailors abroad. In retrospect, I’m proud of the USA for giving         seven stories, most of which were also in my old 1942 book.
our fighting force such interesting entertainment, instead of          But, at least I could now read them without damaging the
whatever some government board deemed a “classic.”                     antique tattered copy. Occasionally, too, I’d find a lone
   But, in any case, I looked over the book and saw that a lot of      Lovecraft story in a horror anthology.
the stories were pretty short, plus they sounded scary. As a kid,         My search for Lovecraft continued. Also, in 1969, I
those were my two criteria for reading. The very first story I ever    discovered that the local college library had a full set of first-
read by Lovecraft was “Pickman’s Model,” which blew me away. It        edition Lovecraft hardbacks, so of course, I checked them
was amazing. The second story I read was “The Outsider,” which I       out. Every few weeks, I’d check them out again. I was the only
didn’t fully understand—I couldn’t figure out what the last sentence   person who ever read them, as I could easily tell by looking at
meant for another three years! But, again, I loved the story—the       the check-out slip in the pocket in the back of the book. But,
eerie milieu, the bizarre happenings, amazed me. I was hooked. I       I did get to read all of Lovecraft’s fiction.
kept reading, and pretty soon I’d finished the entire book.               I loved it all and was particularly fascinated with Great
                                                                       Cthulhu. I tried to get a Vac-U-Form, having convinced
                                                                       myself I could use it to make a cool plastic figure of Cthulhu. I
                                                                       was wrong, but man, I really wanted plastic Lovecraft figures.
                                                                       I was already using Airfix figures to do tabletop wargames
                                                                       with my friends, and adding a monster would be so cool.
                                                                          Anyway, in 1973, the college belatedly realized that the
                                                                       Lovecraft first editions were valuable, so locked them away—
                                                                       inaccessible to mere high schoolers. But, fortune was with
                                                                       me, because in that same year Ballantine finally released new
                                                                       paperback editions of Lovecraft’s stories, and I was able to
                                                                       buy them ALL. It had been a long, hard decade of searching
                                                                       and researching and agonizing. I finally had Lovecraft on my
                                                                       bookshelf in a fairly complete version.
                                                                          This lengthy quest for Lovecraft doubtless is much of the
                                                                       reason I am still so obsessed with this author. Consider in all
                                                                       this time every single person I met who knew about Lovecraft
                                                                       had learned about him through my proselytization. I never
                                                                       met anyone who’d found Lovecraft on their own, and this
                                                                       remained true throughout my college years, up to 1980. My
                                                                       experience was probably impossible to reproduce today. I
                                                                       read the story “The Call of Cthulhu” for the first time without
                                                                       knowing who or what a Cthulhu was. I had no idea that it was
                                                                       a creature or that it would be of titanic size. The story’s final
                                                                       revelation was a fearful surprise.

                         Sandy Petersen

   The Chaosium team thought the 1920s was a fabulous era           good or ill all over the world.
and swallowed the concept hook, line, and sinker. Instead of           In 1988 I finally left Chaosium—not because of any dispute,
worrying about whether Lovecraft was a great author, they           but simply I sought a better-paying job in the video game
instead focused on the fascinating setting and did their best       industry. I found it and eventually was involved in the design

times that it was easy to adapt the adventures to a modern          many millions of copies. Call of Cthulhu even today has a
setting or vice-versa to adapt a modern-age idea to the 1920s.      much smaller reach, spread over 40 years. But, it is by far more
Now, of course, Call of Cthulhu has moved into new eras, such       beloved than my digital success. I am often asked to attend as
as the Victorian age, Dark Ages, the distant future, and the        a guest of honor at conventions around the world—so many
that I am forced to turn down most offers. But, this is never
ever due to my work on Doom or Age of Empires. It’s invariably
because the organizers know me through Call of Cthulhu or,
sometimes, my later board games.
   Lovecraft and Cthulhu have greatly affected my life. I
owe everything to them. My family has been raised around
Lovecraft. I own a life-sized brass bust of Lovecraft. I have an
original oil painting of Cthulhu on my living room wall—in
the spot that a more normal family might have a painting of
Elvis or a seascape. Though, I suppose my Cthulhu painting
is a seascape, so to speak.
   And, I have followed in Greg Stafford’s footsteps. He
founded Chaosium in order to get his games published,
and I have now done the same, founding Petersen Games.
Though I am no longer an employee of Chaosium, I am their
friend and they are mine. I exult in their successes. Best of
all, my new company, primarily a maker of board games,
creates plastic figures of Lovecraftian horrors! So now, at last,
I have my toy Cthulhu after over fifty years of pining for it.
   And, it’s all due to Call of Cthulhu—all that I am I owe to
it. Thank you, Greg, Chaosium, and Howard.

   Eventual death or madness also meant that “losing” wasn’t      Call of Cthulhu its identifiable flavor and play style, making

and one that made Sandy’s game unique in many ways. It               Third, the rules were surprisingly complex, robust, and

and an enduring peril; not something encountered in the           The initial characters were also functional right out of
plural to be slaughtered again and again. The characters          character generation.
would always remain puny and vulnerable.                             With the Sanity rules, character power and knowledge
                                                                  came with an absolute limit. Eventual total sanity loss or
                                                                  death as an expected and anticipated part of play made it
                                                                  important to be able to quickly roll up a new character, have
                                                                  a handle on them and have the character playable from the
                                                                  beginning. There was always a cousin, a nephew, or a private
                                                                  investigator going through the deceased or insane’s things, to
                                                                  jump into the campaign to keep the flow of the game going.
                                                                     This was an important combination. Plugging exploits
                                                                  made the gameplay and character use more fluid and stable.
                                                                  It also meant that no character was going to build up to levels
                                                                  of unstoppable or trans-human power. Combined with the
                                                                  character generation system, this meant that the death of a
                                                                  character or two or six at one time in the campaign did not
                                                                  mean that the play had to come to a full stop until the new
                                                                  characters “leveled up.” It also meant that play did not come
                                                                  to a grinding halt as new characters were created in some
                                                                  time-consuming roadblock. Both play and campaigns could
                                                                  roll right along without needing a power curve.
                                                                     The fourth and final development was keeping the initial
                                                                  game in the historic setting of the Mythos, as written by
                                                                  Lovecraft. This was combined with the level of quality that
                                                                  the published scenarios and related material had. Instead of
                                                                  players facing a random dungeon in the ground, campaigns
                                                                  had fully fleshed out settings, props, real-world locations,
                                                                  and timelines. This meant that there was a recognizable

Every Friday evening, my mate Phill and I would fight goblins       of play, making it easy to get on the same page as other
in the canteen. Our school uniforms were our armor, and             gamers. One of the selling points for me is that mode of play.

of H. P. Lovecraft or Cthulhu. Looking at the articles, it was

   Fast forward a few years, and I had left school and moved
into a house by the sea, along with that same friend. He
recommended that I read a story by H. P. Lovecraft. So,
that night, I lay in bed and leafed through his Grafton
paperback, settling on “Pickman’s Model.” I’ve never been a
horror aficionado, but, at the same time, there’s always been
something in horror stories and films that has intrigued
me. The story told of lost streets, an underground pit, and
a tormented artist. I was genuinely scared by it. Within the
week, I was walking home from the local game store with
a secondhand boxed set of Call of Cthulhu. Never having
played the game before, and having only one player, I wanted
to start with something simple, and so I found the scenario
“Paper Chase” in the Cthulhu Companion.
   Phill and I sat in our lounge one afternoon and played the
scenario through. Here was something different. For a start,
Phill’s character was just a normal guy. He didn’t have a sword
or spells. He wasn’t a secret government agent. Also, while the
scenario featured some horror tropes, such as a graveyard and a
character that was no longer human, was it really a horror story?
This was the start of a new chapter in my roleplaying experience.                           Paul Fricker

Lynne Hardy
I had absolutely no idea who Lovecraft was, or what the
words “cosmic horror” meant when I first encountered the
Call of Cthulhu roleplaying game back in the early 1990s.
There were a couple of reasons for this. For a start, I didn’t
really do horror. Yes, I had managed to blag my way into
a showing of Gremlins at the local pictures when I wasn’t
actually old enough to see it. And, I had nearly bought a
copy of Christine (or was it Carrie?) when I was stuck in a
tiny Cornish village on holiday one summer as a pre-teen
when the weather took a severe turn for the worse. But, on
the whole, me and horror just didn’t really get on—I was far
too afraid of the dark back then for that to happen. Plus, I
was mostly into horses and dragons and British mythology
during my formative years, having been brought up on a
steady diet of weird and wonderful stories by E. Nesbitt,
Susan Cooper, and various BBC adaptations of things like
The Moon Stallion and The Box of Delights. And Doctor Who,
of course.
   So, when John, the GM who first introduced me to
roleplaying via RuneQuest (and Shirley the Dwarf when I
went to university), came in from Games Workshop with a                                     Lynne Hardy
   To be honest, I never expected to go back to it, other              I’ve been a part of The Chaosium for 25 years now. I have
than playing the odd game at conventions for old time’s             worn a lot of hats during that time. My business cards used
sake. It was all rather a happy accident that I ended up            to be something of a hit at trade shows. My roles were always
being drawn back into the fold by Achtung! Cthulhu                  changing and my cards were almost collectible. One thing I
(Modiphius Entertainment) after my husband, Richard,                have not done much of is write for Call of Cthulhu. Oh sure,
had a conversation with Sarah Newton about World War II             I helped develop projects, playtested, proofread, advertised,
horror gaming at Dragonmeet, back when it was still held at         marketed, sold, played, ran, and generally promoted
Kensington Town Hall in London. Not long after, having quit         Call of Cthulhu for ages. I’ve done some art direction,
my day job as a college lecturer to become a freelance games        assignments, and ghostwriting here and there. Nothing

   The rest, as they say, is history. And now, as Call of Cthulhu   scenes one. From the shadows, I have influenced… Oh, who

Dustin Wright
Howdy Folks. If you have been part of the Chaosium Tribe
long enough you might recognize me as one of those Cthulhu
Masters guys, that dude from the convention booth, the Cult
of Chaos creator, the deep but oddly reassuring voice on the
phone, the owner of hundreds of tiny Pixie Mammoth, the
Gameshop support person, Chaosium’s Distributor sales
cowboy, the Spider Keeper, The Starry Wisdom editor, that
fellow who answers all my emails, The R’lyeh Report author,
the one who used to buy us all pizza before GenCon, the
convention prize support wrangler, the Chaosium staffer who
lived up in the mountains and went a bit feral, the Chaosium
blogger, that strange guy who wrote all those BRP Gold
Book shipping updates, the person always trying to get me
to run demonstration games at my local shop or convention,
that guy you contact when none of the Californians are
answering your emails, the Hurricane Harvey refugee, the
Texan who lost his power for a week that winter all of Texas
dropped off the grid, or just good old Dustin O’Chaosium.
If you are new to the Chaosium Tribe: Howdy! I’m Dustin. I
was rather hoping we’d finally meet.

                                                                                             Dustin Wright

                                                          Mike Mason
                                         Foreword
                What has risen may sink, and what is sunk may rise… Cthulhu still lives.
                                                                                       —H. P. Lovecraft
My own introduction to H. P. Lovecraft was as a child, when I found a tattered book of stories, printed
long before I was born. I read that book in bed that night, and became entranced forever. If you, too, love
Lovecraft’s stories, you can now experience the Cthulhu Mythos in a whole new way.
   I’ve been a game designer more than 30 years, and a Lovecraft fan far longer. I’ve been an author, a
college professor, and even executive producer on a movie. You’ve probably heard of some of the games I
helped to create, and might even have played them. In the video game field, I’m probably best known for
the strategy games I worked on. But I am most proud of how I helped create the horror roleplaying genre,
as original author/developer of the Call of Cthulhu game way back in 1980.
   My other ventures have, at times, been successful. Some of the computer games I helped to develop
sold millions of copies. But when I am invited to a game convention as a guest, or when a fan offers to
buy me a drink, or asks for an autograph, or any of the perks that my modest fame has accumulated, it is
always for Call of Cthulhu, never one of my other games. Call of Cthulhu is beloved in a way nothing else
has been—and this is of course due mostly to the power of Lovecraft’s world, and Chaosium’s dedication
in bringing it forth to you, my friends.
   Chaosium, through Call of Cthulhu, popularized the terrifying universe of H. P. Lovecraft in gaming
and made Cthulhu a household word. Now, Chaosium has put together a team of experienced game
professionals with decades of experience. In this 7th edition, Chaosium has managed once again a
successful combination of roleplaying adventure with Lovecraftian horror, both of which are dear to my
heart. They are, and were, the right team for this topic.
   Hardcore fans will be thrilled to hear that the new edition has lost nothing in the transition, and teems
with terror, horror, and ways to introduce you and your friends to the dread world that H. P. Lovecraft
and his friends constructed.

											 Sandy Petersen
												          2014

If you’ve never played a roleplaying game before, you might
still be wondering just how it all works. The following         The players agree to the plan.
example of play provides an illustration of a typical gaming
session. Don’t worry about some of the terms used, as you’ll    PAULA: I’ll keep watch from the street.
become better acquainted with them as you read the rest of
this book (there’s also a useful Glossary on page 386).         ARNOLD: I’ll stay in the car for now and keep watch—my
     This example game uses the classic 1920s setting. The      investigator is still very nervous.
investigators are trying to find out why Boss Morgan, a
notorious gangland figure, has vanished.                        JOE: I’ll go with Cathy.
     Paula, Joe, Cathy and Arnold are the players, each of
them controlling an investigator. Garrie is in the role of      (GARRIE) KEEPER: Cathy and Joe, make Listen rolls.
Keeper and is running the game (leading the story and
controlling the non-player characters and monsters in the       CATHY: (rolls percentage dice) I succeeded!
game). Notice that while the players have different ways of
referring to their characters, Garrie the Keeper easily sorts   JOE: I failed the roll. So, Cathy, what did you hear?
out their statements and feels no need for consistency.
We join the game in mid-session…                                (GARRIE) KEEPER: You hear a creak of wood; sounds like
                                                                the house’s front door quietly opening. Don’t forget to tick
                                                                your Listen skill, as you got a success.

                                                                CATHY: Right. (She makes a check mark on her sheet). I’m
                                                                grabbing Joe and attempting to hide behind the garbage cans.

                                                                (GARRIE) KEEPER: I’ll need you both to make a Stealth roll.

                                                                JOE and CATHY: Yeah! We both rolled successes (both tick
                                                                their Stealth skills). We’re well hidden behind the garbage.

                                                                (GARRIE) KEEPER: Great. Since it’s night and there are
                                                                no working street lamps near, you can’t see much. The form
                                                                of a hulking figure leaves the house and creeps towards the
                                                                street. When it reaches the middle of the street, it pulls up
                                                                a manhole cover and drops down inside. You hear a splash.
                                                                (Garrie makes a dubious liquid-sound…)

                                                                CATHY: Did he lock the door behind him?

                                                                (GARRIE) KEEPER: Not only is it not locked, it’s wide open.

                                                                CATHY: I’ll sneak back and tell the others.

                                                                    their games, such as statuettes, letters, books or even clay
                                                                    tablets. Such items, if prepared with care, can lend drama
                                                                    and atmosphere to a game. However, they can also end
                                                                    up looking silly if they are poorly presented. Some players
                                                                    cannot believe that a polystyrene block is anything other
                                                                    than a polystyrene block; even through the Keeper has spent
                                                                    hours carving it to resemble the “Black Obelisk of Thantos”!
                                                                    The best way to decide if props will work with your group is
                                                                    to talk about it and agree whether you all feel they are worth
                                                                    the time and effort required.

Dice Roll Variations
Sometimes a dice notation in the rules or in a scenario is
preceded by a number; it tells the reader that more than one
such die should be rolled and that their results should be
added together. For instance, 2D6 means that two 6-sided
dice should be rolled and totalled (or roll a D6 twice and add
the scores together).
    Sometimes additions are shown to die rolls. You might
see 1D6+1, for instance. This means that the number
following the plus sign should be added to the result of the
D6 roll. For 1D6+1, the result must be 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7.
    A notation may require that different dice be rolled at one
time. If a monster claws for 1D6+1+2D4 damage, find the
power of the actual attack by rolling the three requested dice,
totalling the results, and adding one (thus, rolling 1D6 and
2D4 [1D4 and another 1D4] and adding 1 to the total rolled).
The notation “damage bonus” (or “DB”) appended to attack
damage reminds the Keeper and players to add the monster’s
or investigator’s “damage bonus” to the dice rolled.

                                    chapter 2: h.p. Lovecraft

                                                                  of Hali near the city Carcosa on a planet circling the star
                                                                  Aldebaran. Although briefly mentioned by Lovecraft in
                                                                  early tales, these were actually the creations of Ambrose
                                                                  Bierce (1842-circa 1914), an American journalist and early
                                                                  exponent of the weird tale. Bierce was an early influence on
                                                                  HPL, as were a number of other writers. His favorite author
                                                                  was always Edgar Allan Poe (1809-1849), and Poe’s influence
                                                                  can be clearly seen in some of Lovecraft’s first adult fiction.
                                                                  “The Outsider” (1921) perhaps most closely emulates Poe’s
                                                                  style and subject matter. Arthur Machen (1863-1947) was
                                                                  another early influence; his story “The Great God Pan”
                                                                  (1894) is very similar in theme to Lovecraft’s “The Dunwich
                                                                  Horror”. Robert W. Chambers (1865-1933) also left his mark
                                                                  on Lovecraft. A mysterious play, The King in Yellow, figures in
                                                                  some of Chambers’ stories and probably inspired Lovecraft
                                                                  to create the Necronomicon, the Pnakotic Manuscripts and
                                                                  the other tomes of eldritch lore for which his tales are
                                                                  famous. It was Chambers who first borrowed from Bierce
                                                                  the Lake of Hali and Carcosa, perhaps inspiring Lovecraft to
                                                                  attempt transpositions.
                                                                      Lord Dunsany (1878-1957) was probably Lovecraft’s
                                                                  strongest contemporary influence. It was Dunsany’s The
                                                                  Gods of Pegana (1905) that encouraged Lovecraft to write
                                                                  several dream-based stories and first provided him with the
         The Master of American Horror, H.P. Lovecraft            idea of an artificial pantheon of gods. Algernon Blackwood,
Unaussprechlichen Kulten and the mad poet Justin Geoffrey,        another contemporary admired by HPL, drew upon Native
author of the terrible People of the Monolith. These were also    American legends for the version of the Wendigo later
incorporated into Lovecraft’s stories, along with references to   adapted into the Mythos by August Derleth, which is there
Howard’s prehistoric Cimmeria.                                    called Ithaqua.

         The dreaded Necronomicon, one of Lovecraft’s most
     famous creations, undergoes a gradual evolution. First
     mentioned in “The Hound” (1922), it is here attributed to
     an Abdul Alhazred, an Arab poet mentioned in an earlier
     story, “The Nameless City” (1921). Alhazred was, in fact,
     the boyhood persona of a five-year-old HPL, his youthful

       About This Chapter                                              The Steps
                                                                       Here are the five steps to creating an investigator:
                   n Call of Cthulhu, each player takes the role
                   of an investigator, someone whose mission is          G Step One: Generate Characteristics
                   to unearth hidden secrets, discover forgotten
                   places and, armed with knowledge which                G Step Two: Determine Occupation
                   man was not meant to know, stand against the          G Step Three: Decide Skills and allocate Skill Points
     bloodcurdling horrors of the Cthulhu Mythos.
         Working as a team, investigators can come from                  G Step Four: Create a backstory
     disparate backgrounds and be of varied occupations—each
     bringing certain expertise to the group. Together, joined in
                                                                         G Step Five: Equip investigator
     comradeship and common purpose, you will stand steadfast
     against the coming darkness.

                                                Call of Cthulhu

          EDU is a factor in determining how many Occupational
     skill points (see Occupation Skills page 36) an investigator
     begins with, and represents the investigator’s starting
     percentage for the Own Language skill (see page 67). EDU
     is also used when making Know rolls (see Chapter 5: Game
     System).
          Note: From this point forward, any references to a
     characteristic are to the full value (dice roll multiplied by
     five). Any adjustments are made to that value.

     Luck
     When creating an investigator roll 3D6 and multiply by 5
     for a Luck score. See Chapter 5: Game System for how Luck
     is used.

     Age
     A player can choose any age between 15 and 90 for their
     investigator. If you wish to create an investigator outside
     this age range, it is up to the Keeper to adjudicate. Use the
     appropriate modifiers for your chosen age only (they are not
     cumulative).
       G 15 to 19 years old: Deduct 5 points among STR
            and SIZ. Deduct 5 points from EDU. Roll twice to
            generate a Luck score (see Luck, above) and use the
            higher value.
       G 20 to 39 years old: Make an improvement check for EDU.
       G 40 to 49 years old: Make 2 improvement checks for
            EDU and deduct 5 points among STR, CON or                                      The Horror in Clay
            DEX, and reduce APP by 5.
                                                                        Half and Fifth Characteristic Values
       G 50 to 59 years old: Make 3 improvement checks for              Once the percentage values for each characteristic have
            EDU and deduct 10 points among STR, CON or                  been determined, the next step is to write in the half and
            DEX, and reduce APP by 10.                                  fifth values for each characteristic on the investigator sheet,
                                                                        entering the values alongside the percentage.
       G 60 to 69 years old: Make 4 improvement checks for

       G 80 to 89 years old: Make 4 improvement checks for              fifth values are required, this will be clearly stated, normally

        To make an EDU improvement check, simply roll                   characteristics and skills in the game and all the numbers

Other Attributes
Damage Bonus and Build [STR & SIZ]
All investigators, Keeper-controlled characters and                           Table 1:
monsters have attributes known as "Damage Bonus" (DB)
and Build. Larger and stronger creatures and humans do                 Damage Bonus and Build
more physical damage than their weaker brethren.                            STR + SIZ          Damage Bonus          Build
    To determine damage bonus, add STR to SIZ and look
                                                                              2 — 64                 –2               –2
up the total on Table 1: Damage Bonus and Build. Each
range of results correlates with a die modifier or dice roll.                65 — 84                 –1               –1
Build is determined using the same figures.                                 85 — 124                None               0
    In hand-to-hand combat, add the indicated damage                        125 — 164               +1D4               1
bonus modifier or roll to all the character’s blows, whether
using a natural weapon, such as a fist, or a melee weapon.                  165 — 204               +1D6               2
    Build is used in fighting maneuvers and chases, and also                205 — 284               +2D6               3
to give a sense of scale (see Fighting Maneuvers, page 105                  285 — 364               +3D6               4
and 279).
                                                                            365 — 444               +4D6               5
                                                                            445 — 524              +5D6*              6*
    Someone with a combined STR and SIZ of 134 would
    add 1D4 to hand-to-hand damage rolls, while a                      *Add an additional 1D6 to Damage Bonus and +1 to
    weakling investigator whose combined value is only                 Build for each additional 80 points or fraction thereof.
    70 would deduct 1 point from melee damage.



Note: Damage bonus is not applied to firearms attacks.

Hit Points [CON & SIZ]                                               Step 2:
                                                                     Determine Occupation
Hit points are used to track the cumulative damage inflicted
upon an investigator, non-player character, or monster
during the game, and indicate how long he or she can stay in         An occupation shows how an investigator makes a living, be
the action before collapsing from pain, exhaustion, or death.        it as a doctor, a student, or lowlife fraudster. An occupation
    Figure out the character’s hit point total by adding CON and     also reflects a particular investigator’s field of expertise, and
SIZ, then dividing the total by ten (rounding down any fractions).   so dictates which of their skills should be higher.
    The investigator sheet is designed to help the player keep            The actual occupation of your investigator will have
track of hit points and wounds. Enter the investigator’s Hit         limited effect during the game; it is simply a basis for your
Point Total in the box marked "Hit Points". For more details on      investigator’s starting skills and helping to define his or her
hit points and wounds, see Wounds and Healing (page 119).            backstory. Some occupations are typically Lovecraftian:
                                                                     Antiquarian, Author, Dilettante, Doctor of Medicine,
Movement Rate (MOV)                                                  Journalist, Police Detective, and Professor. Other occupations
An investigator can move a number of yards (or meters) up            are not those you would normally find in a Lovecraft story,
to five times their MOV value in one round.                          however they may interest particular players and be fun to
                                                                     play in a Call of Cthulhu game.
   If both DEX and STR are each less than SIZ: MOV 7                      An occupation ties together a cluster of skills.
   If either STR or DEX is equal to or greater                       For instance, in the sample occupations, Antiquarian
   than SIZ, or if all three are equal:           MOV 8              encompasses: Appraise, Art/Craft, History, Library Use,
   If both STR and DEX are each greater than SIZ: MOV 9              Other Language, one interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk,
                                                                     Intimidate, or Persuade), Spot Hidden, and one other skill of
   If age is in the 40s:        deduct 1 from MOV                    the player’s choice (to reflect something special or relevant
   If age is in the 50s:        deduct 2 from MOV                    about the investigator’s past). Some occupations include no
   If age is in the 60s:        deduct 3 from MOV                    free choices; others may have two or more.
   If age is in the 70s:        deduct 4 from MOV                         Once you have chosen the occupation for your
   If age is in the 80s:        deduct 5 from MOV                    investigator, write it down on the investigator sheet and then
                                                                     make a note of the occupation's professional skills.
Note: Do not apply these MOV rules to non-humans.                         Sample occupations can be found on page 40.

      Create a Backstory
       Think about your investigator and write in a
       few pithy entries for three to six for: Personal
      Description, Ideology/Beliefs, Significant People,
      Meaningful Locations, Treasured Possessions,
      and Traits. It’s not essential to have an entry for
      each category, but the more you are able to define,
      the more your investigator comes to life. Use the
      tables on pages 42–45 for inspiration. Pick one
      entry and underline or star it (*) to show that it is

                  What the Numbers Mean
                    Strength                                                      Appearance
0: Enfeebled: unable to even stand up or lift a cup of tea.     0: So unsightly, others are affected by fear, revulsion, or pity.
15: Puny, weak.                                                 15: Ugly, possibly disfigured due to injury or at birth.
50: Average human strength.                                     50: Average human appearance.
90: One of the strongest people you’ve ever met.                90: One of the most beautiful people you could meet, natural
99: World class (Olympic weightlifter). Human maximum.          magnetism.
140: Beyond human strength (gorilla or horse).                  99*: The height of glamour and cool (supermodel or world
200+: Monstrous strength (e.g. Gla'aki, see Chapter 14:         renowned film star). Human maximum.
Monsters, Beasts and Alien Gods)                                Note: *APP is normally used only for humans, and does not
                                                                exceed 99.
                 Constitution
0: Dead.                                                                           Intelligence
1: Sickly, prone to prolonged illness and probably unable       0: No intellect, unable to comprehend the world around them.
to operate without assistance.                                  15: Slow learner, able to undertake only the most basic
15: Weak health, prone to bouts of ill health, great            math, or read beginner-level books.
propensity for feeling pain.                                    50: Average human intellect.
50: Average healthy human.                                      90: Quick-witted, probably able to comprehend multiple
90: Shrugs off colds, hardy and hale.                           languages or theorems.
99: iron constitution, able to withstand great amounts of       99: Genius (Einstein, Da Vinci, Tesla, etc.). Human maximum.
pain. Human maximum.                                            140: Beyond human intellect (e.g. Elder Things, see
140: Beyond human constitution (e.g. elephant).                 Chapter 14: Monsters, Beasts and Alien Gods).

diseases (e.g. Nyogtha, see Chapter 14: Monsters,               operate in multiple dimensions (e.g. Great Cthulhu, see
Beasts and Alien Gods).                                         Chapter 14: Monsters, Beasts and Alien Gods).

                                                  Call of Cthulhu

         Second, they can be called upon during the investigator
     development phase for the recovery of sanity points (see The
     Investigator Development Phase, page 94).
         Third, and perhaps most importantly of all, the
     corruption of the investigator’s background mirrors their
     loss of sanity and growing knowledge of the Mythos, as
     all that was once held dear becomes meaningless. During
     episodes of insanity, or as a result of major wounds, the
     Keeper may add or amend a background entry. Ultimately
     the investigator’s only connections to the world are madness
     and pain.

     When crafting background entries, be specific,
     emotional and emphatic
     Try to phrase each entry to emphasize its personal nature.
     For example, “my wife” identifies the subject, but tells us
     nothing about the nature of the relationship.
       A) Specify a name. Name the person or place, identify
          the item or concept.
       B) Attach a feeling. Start with positive or negative and
          build from there.
         Make it intense, use love instead of like, use despise instead
     of dislike. Be emphatic; use must in place of would prefer. Try to
     phrase it in a way that makes it personal and meaningful.                     "Dear, did you hear something outside?"


         Consider "wife" as a starting point. Now give her a
         name, 'My wife, Annabel". Now be emotional, “My be-                  You might be creating a male investigator in his twenties,
         loved wife, Annabel". Now be emphatic, "My beloved                   with no thought of any family. Rolling for ‘significant
         wife, Annabel. I couldn’t live without her". That short              people’ indicates a child. You may not have considered
         journey delivers a lot more information and context.                 that your character has a child, but why not? Becoming
         Of course it could have been developed differently,                  a father may not have been intentional. Consider the
         consider: "My wife Annabel, who makes my life a                      options; you may have had an affair with a married
         misery", "My poor wife Annabel; if only we could have                woman, your wife may have died and your child fostered,
         a child together".                                                   or you may be happily married with a family.



     Using Random Tables for Inspiration                                   Personal Description
     Sometimes coming up with background details on the fly can            Think of a distinct look that sums up your investigator’s
     be difficult. The following random tables provide a way to            appearance (APP).
     quickly determine some background details—you can either
     roll on the tables or simply pick ones that seem suitable, or            Here are some possibilities (choose rather than roll):
     just use the lists for inspiration.
         As appropriate, roll 1D10 on each of the following charts           Rugged                 Handsome                  Ungainly
     to select an entry for each category. Each option has some               Pretty                Glamorous                Baby-faced
     examples to get you started. Don’t feel constrained to use what          Smart                   Untidy                    Dull
     you roll; if it doesn’t mesh with your character concept then roll       Dirty                  Dazzler                  Bookish
     again or choose a different option. However, don’t dismiss it out      Youthful                  Weary                    Plump
     of hand; sometimes something that is at first incongruous will           Stout                   Hairy                     Slim
     add an unexpected but welcome twist to a character.                     Elegant                 Scruffy                   Stocky
         Above all, be clear that the random tables are there as a             Pale                   Sullen                  Ordinary
     springboard for your imagination. Each dice roll will give               Rosy                   Tanned                   Wrinkled
     you something from that realm of life to react to; embrace               Stuffy                  Mousy                    Sharp
     it or reject it, but be sure to react to it and use it to help fuel     Brawny                   Dainty                  Muscular
     your imagination. Weave the background together to make                Strapping                 Gawky                     Frail
     a credible all-round character.

                  his chapter looks at skills in detail, providing
                  definitions of their use and scope within the
                  game. Each category of skill encompasses a
                  range of possibilities and, to ensure brevity,
                                                                               Skill Points:
                  descriptions have to be general summaries of
                  intent and coverage.
                                                                            What Do They Mean?
                                                                                   General level of ability
     Skill Definitions                                                                by skill value:
     Skills represent what is known within a certain era, and some        01%–05%: Novice: complete amateur.
     skills are tagged [Modern] to denote that they can only be
     used in modern-day settings. Some skills are given a generic         06%–19%: Neophyte: beginner with a small amount
     name, which may not be appropriate for some settings; for            of knowledge.
     example, Drive Auto would not be suitable for a game set in
     Victorian London, and should be reworded appropriately—              20%–49%: Amateur: possesses some talent or rudi-
     in this case as Drive Carriage.                                      mentary training (hobby level).
         Skill percentiles are not proportions of what is
     hypothetically knowable. If they were able to stack their            50%–74%: Professional: allows a character to eke
     respective knowledge on a table like poker chips and measure         out a living from the skill. Equivalent to a bachelor’s
     the difference, a physicist of 60% in the modern day knows           degree in a specific subject.
     much more than a physicist of 90% skill in 1910.
         Equally, some skills would be affected by location. A            75%–89%: Expert: advanced expertise. Corre-
     Japanese investigator might have a Law skill of 75% in Japan;        sponds with a master’s degree or Ph.D.
     however if the same investigator were tested on Spanish
     law then the Keeper would probably increase the level of             90%+: Master: among the world’s best in the skill.
     difficulty of the roll.
         A skill level of 50% is high enough to let a character eke
     out a living from it. If an investigator rises high in a skill   In the case of Art and Craft, Science, and Survival, these skills
     unrelated to their profession, player and Keeper could confer    encompass a wide diversity of specializations. The Keeper

54
                                            chapter 4: skills

                                                                  Pushing Skills
                                                                  For each skill, a number of examples are provided for what
                                                                  might constitute a "pushed roll" (see page 84 for Pushing the
                                                                  Roll), and also some of the possible consequences for failing
                                                                  a pushed roll. The suggestions given should be viewed as
                                                                  simply that—suggestions. The latitude for what could justify
                                                                  a pushed roll is wide, and should be best determined by
                                                                  actions, motivations, and events within the game. Likewise,
                                                                  the consequences of a failed pushed roll will be best served if
                                                                  inspiration is drawn from current game events, non-player
                                                                  characters, and the game world.
                                                                      Likewise, where appropriate, an example is provided for
                                                                  a possible consequence of an investigator pushing a skill roll
                                                                  while insane. The very fact that the investigator is insane
                                                                  while performing a task heightens the stakes for the roll,
                                                                  as the consequences of a pushed failure will often be all the
                                                                  more extreme (or bizarre).
                                                                      The examples merely provide ideas, and it is intended
                                                                  that both players and Keepers fashion their own justifications
                                                                  and consequences as appropriate to their games and playing
                                                                  styles.

                                                                  Combined Skill Rolls
          Harvey attempts to repair an electric turbine.          In some situations the Keeper may ask for a roll against more

most extreme and dire situations. Sometimes this may be
simply that the performance of the skill is greatly hindered,
such as when being shot at or pursued, or when conditions
are uniquely appalling.                                               A deranged cultist suddenly draws a gun on Harvey.

         If an insane investigator fails a pushed roll, he or she
     could destroy the item, believing it to be cursed; alternatively
     they regard the item as their personal salvation and refuse to
     give it up to anyone else.

     Archaeology (01%)
     Allows dating and identification of artifacts from past
     cultures, and the detection of fakes. Ensures expertise in
     setting up and excavating a dig site. On inspecting a site,
     the user might deduce the purposes and way of life of
     those who left the remains. Anthropology might aid in this.
     Archaeology also helps identify written forms of extinct
     human languages.
     Opposing skill/Difficulty level:
      i Regular difficulty: sufficient time and resources to
          do the job properly.
       i Hard difficulty: a lack of time and/or limited resources.
     Pushing examples: taking more time to study the site or item;
     conducting further research; consulting another expert.

     Sample Consequences of failing a Pushed roll: The site is
     spoiled, with finds ruined through incompetence, vandalism
     or theft; some higher authority seizes the site or the finds               "These aren't any hieroglyphs that I know of."
     from your control; publicity leads to the finds being stolen.

The user is able to provide emergency medical care. This
might encompass: applying a splint to a broken leg, stemming      Pushing examples: taking more time to examine; con-
bleeding, treating a burn, resuscitating a drowning victim,       ducting further research; consulting another expert; hiring

to treat diseases (where the Medicine skill is required).         at a huge cost.

hour, in which case it grants 1 hit point. It may be attempted    Sample Consequences of failing a Pushed roll: consul-
once, with subsequent attempts constituting a Pushed roll.        tations alert your adversaries, who become aware of your

with a success granted if either one of them rolls a success.     much time and money is wasted on fruitless research.

to consciousness. A character is limited to one successful        is convinced that they are somehow displaced in time, or
treatment of both First Aid and Medicine until further            perhaps they start believing they are living in a historic

         Once an item has been thrown, it is quite literally out of
     the investigator’s hands and therefore there is nothing the
     player can do to justify Pushing the roll. If multiple missiles
     are available, there is no risk connected with failure and it       Specializations:
     is only a matter of time until the investigator is successful,      Transferable Skill Benefit

     cession hoping one will hit the target; taking a massive run-up           to use a firearm, there are more similarities than
     and risking your momentum taking you over the ravine.                     differences between the subdivisions of firearms.

78
                                          chapter 4: skills

Languages
When a character first raises a language (other than Own
Language) to 50%, all other related language families are
raised by 10 percentage points (but not higher than 50%).
This may happen only once more: when a character first
raises a language (other than Own Language) to 90%, all
other related languages within that family are raised by 10
percentage points (but not higher than 90%). For example,
related family categories include Germanic (English,
German, Dutch), Slavic (Russian, Czech, Polish, etc.), Niger-
Congo (Swahili, Zulu, Yoruba, etc.)
    Note this is in no way an exhaustive list of the possible
language families, and Keepers opting to use this rule are
recommended to refer to standard encyclopedia entries for
“language families.”

     road. There’s a disagreement, a conflict—a reason to roll dice       Determining the Difficulty Level
     and see whether the investigator’s car is indeed pushed into         The Keeper determines the difficulty level for skill rolls.
     the ditch. The story has reached a moment of tension that            There are three levels of difficulty in Call of Cthulhu: Regular,
     requires a definite outcome. Time to reach for the dice!             Hard, and Extreme.
                                                                          Regular difficulty level: an average example of what would
     Skill Rolls                                                          challenge a competent person. The player needs to roll equal
                                                                          to or below their full skill or characteristic in order to suc-
     The word "roll" is used in conjunction with the appropriate          ceed. This should be used in the vast majority of cases.
     skill or characteristic; thus the Keeper will, for example, call
     for a "Climb roll" or a "Strength roll".                             Hard difficulty level: this task would challenge a profession-
         The Keeper has the final say about which skill or                al. The player needs to roll equal to or below a half of their
     characteristic is appropriate to roll, depending on the              skill or characteristic in order to succeed. This should only
     situation and the nature of the opposition. The term "skill          be used occasionally.
     roll" is used regardless of whether a skill or a characteristic
     is used.

82
                                       chapter 5: game system

Extreme difficulty level: this task would challenge an ex-
pert; it is on the border of what is humanly possible. The
player needs to roll equal to or below a fifth of their skill or
characteristic in order to succeed. This should be reserved
for extreme situations.
     If you, as Keeper, feel that the task is easy then don’t ask
for a dice roll. Save dice rolls for challenging situations.
     If the investigator is facing a living being, the difficulty
level should be set according to the complementary skill or
characteristic belonging to that being (human or otherwise).
It is up to the Keeper to decide which skill or characteristic
would be used by the opponent to counter the investigator’s
actions—examples can be found with each skill entry (see
Chapter 4: Skills).
   i If the opponent’s skill or characteristic is below 50,
         the difficulty level is Regular.
  i If the opponent’s skill or characteristic is equal to or
       above 50, the difficulty level is Hard.
  i If the opponent’s skill or characteristic is equal to or
       above 90, the difficulty level is Extreme.

         Harvey drives his car directly at a shambling human-
         like monster with the goal of hitting it. The Keeper
         declares that the difficulty level to hit it will be hard
         (given it has a Dodge skill of 60). The player rolls less
         than half of her investigator’s Drive Auto skill—a suc-
         cess! The player describes the scene...
             “I push down the accelerator and ram into the
         shambling thing, knocking it flying. I brake hard, spin
         the car to a halt and jump out, leaving the engine
         running. I draw my pistol and fire three shots into the
         monster’s corpse.”
             There’s no need to take issue with the player’s
         description of Harvey’s cool driving technique—it is
         just description—but did he really kill the monster?
         The Keeper checks the monster’s hit points and
         considers that it would take more than this to kill it.
         The player’s goal was to hit the monster with the car
         (which she did), not to kill it. The Keeper jumps in,
         slightly altering what the player has said...
             “Great, but when you get out of your car and look
         around there’s no sign of a body; the thing you hit
         with your car is nowhere to be seen.”
             Alternatively, the Keeper (referring to Other
         Forms of Damage on page 123) could ask the player                              Harvey speeds towards the monster.
         to roll dice to see exactly how much damage the car

    Harvey is fleeing from pursuing cultists. His player
    declares that Harvey is trying to hide behind a mar-
    ket stall positioned in the main street. The Keeper
    determines that this requires a Stealth roll at Regular
    difficulty. Harvey’s player rolls high and fails the roll.
    The Keeper describes the stall as being little more
    than a few boxes on the pavement, with nowhere
    to hide behind. Believing it’s only a matter of time
    before he is caught, Harvey decides to try to hide in
    plain sight, among the passing crowds, hoping that
    the cultists won’t see him in the throng of people.
    The Keeper approves this for a pushed roll. Harvey is
    taking a bigger risk—he’s effectively making himself
    easier to spot, and so will have no chance of escape if
    the pushed roll is failed.
         Harvey’s player makes a second Stealth roll, this
    time succeeding! Harvey’s quick thinking means that
    he melts away into the crowd. The cultists have lost
    him this time!



                                                                                   "Run! Run for your life!"


                                                                                                                                 85
                                                   Call of Cthulhu


         Using the previous example, Harvey’s player has de-
         clared she would like to try to push the failed Stealth
         roll and described what Harvey is doing to justify this.
         The player then asks the Keeper for the likely conse-
         quence if the pushed roll is failed. The Keeper thinks
         for a moment and says, “Well, as Harvey is trying to
         mingle with the crowd of people in the street—if the
         pushed roll is failed he could well find himself bump-
         ing into one of the pursuing cultists and be caught.”


     Pushed Roll: Success
     The player’s goal is achieved as it would have been for the
     original roll. None of the consequences of failure happen.

     Pushed Roll: Failure
     Failing a pushed roll grants the Keeper free rein over the
     outcome, including damage, Sanity checks, loss of equipment,
     isolation from the rest of the investigators, capture, and so
     on. The Keeper can even incorporate the player’s goal being
     achieved (even though the player failed the pushed roll), but
     at some form of cost.
         When a player fails a pushed roll, he or she is giving the
     Keeper license to make the investigators’ lives more difficult.                   Between a rock and a hard place!
     A consequence should steer the game towards the theme
     of horror, taking the investigators one step nearer to their          i The investigator is taken captive rather than killed.
     doom. A player gets to push a skill roll and, if he or she fails,
     the Keeper gets to push the horror back in that player’s face.        i A deal: rather than killing the investigator, the ene-
                                                                               my offers a pact or deal.

          In the previous example, instead of succeeding in the
                                                                           i Fainting: it is perfectly Lovecraftian for a character
                                                                               to faint, only to awaken later to find themselves
         pushed Stealth roll let's say Harvey failed it. The Keeper            unharmed and the situation changed.
         now has license to make matters far worse for Harvey.
             “As Harvey tries to blend into the crowd, he finds
         himself jostled as he goes against the flow of people. He           Harvey is fleeing through underground tunnels, pur-
         is pushed against a market stall, knocking it and himself           sued by ghouls. He sees an abyss before him. Perhaps
         over (Harvey takes 1 point of damage). As Harvey falls              he can leap across to the other side. He fails his Jump
         to the pavement, fruit falling on his head, the sudden              roll. There is really nothing he can do to push this
         noise of his cry makes the crowd part around him.                   roll. The Keeper could simply describe Harvey falling
         He looks up to see everyone staring at him, including               to his death, which would end the game for Harvey’s
         the cultists who were in pursuit. They quickly shout                player. Alternatively, the Keeper could describe how
         out, “He’s our friend!” as they come forward and grab               Harvey falls and lands in an underground river, later
         Harvey. The crowd of people seem satisfied, and no one              to be washed up, injured and lost at an unknown
         notices the dagger that one the cultists is now holding             location, his flashlight and equipment lost. The first
         to Harvey’s ribs as they manhandle him into an alley.               outcome will bring the game to a sudden end and,

                                                                    01: A Critical Success
                                                                    A roll of 01 means that something beneficial occurs beyond
doing this until the opponent’s characteristic has been

                                                                 Difficulty Levels vs. Bonus/Penalty Dice
                                                                 In the majority of cases, only one person, usually the player,
                                                                 makes a skill roll. Any factors that provide an advantage or
                                                                 disadvantage for the investigator should be factored into the
                                                                 difficulty level set by the Keeper. Bonus dice and penalty dice
                                                                 may be awarded in conjunction with difficulty levels, at the
                                                                 Keeper’s discretion, but doing so should be the exception
                                                                 rather than the rule.
                                                                     In the case of an opposed roll (wherein both sides are
                                                                 rolling in opposition to one another, as in melee combat)
                                                                 difficulty levels are never set. The level of success achieved
                                                                 by one side is, in effect, the level of difficulty that the other
                                                                 side must compete against. If there are factors that provide a
                                                                 distinct advantage or disadvantage for one of the characters
                                                                 involved, the Keeper should grant a bonus die or a penalty
                                                                 die to that player.
                                                                 Skill rolls: Set level of difficulty.

                                                                 Opposed rolls: Award penalty dice or bonus dice.

                                                                 Mechanical Repair and Electrical Repair. The chance of
                                                                 success when making one dice roll and comparing it to both

92
                                   chapter 5: game system

                                                                 (whichever is higher). Thus, if the opposing skill is 50% or
                                                                 higher, a Hard success is required (a roll equal to or below
                                                                 half skill). If the opposing skill is 90% or higher, an Extreme
                                                                 success is required (a roll equal to or below one-fifth of skill).
                                                                 For the vast majority of non-player characters, these skills
                                                                 will be below 50%, and so the difficulty level will be Regular
                                                                 and the player only needs a regular success.
                                                                   i If the non-player character is positively inclined
                                                                        towards the player’s goal, don’t bother rolling dice,
                                                                        but simply have them agree.

                                                                   i If the non-player character lacks strong feelings
                                                                        towards the player’s goal then no modification to the
                                                                        difficulty level is required.

                                                                   i If the non-player character is strongly opposed to
                                                                        the player’s goal, the difficulty is raised by one or two
                                                                        levels.

                                                                   i If the player comes up with something to support
                                                                        their case, such as a substantive argument (if using
                                                                        Persuade), or is holding a weapon and is clearly will-
                                                                        ing to use it (in the case of Intimidate), the Keeper
                                                                        should lower the difficulty by one level.
                                                                     The lowest chance of success requires an Extreme success

                                                                                                                                     95
                                                  Call of Cthulhu


         Harvey wants to buy a 12-gauge shotgun, costing
         $40. This being above his $10 spending limit, he
         reduces his cash by $40 (from $82 to $42).
             Later, Harvey wishes to take a trip to England,
         choosing to travel by sea, first class, costing $120.
         This is beyond Harvey’s available cash. Harvey has
         $1665 tied up in assets, and seeks to realize some
         of that capital by getting a loan from the bank. The
         Keeper agrees to this and decides that it might take a
         week to get the money. Harvey transfers $245 from
         his assets to his cash. From the $245, he spends $120
         on the ticket, leaving $125, which he adds to his
         remaining cash of $42. His cash is now $167, and his
         assets $1400. When he gets to England, his finances
         will function just as they did back home (average
         living standards and a $10 per day spending limit).

                                     chapter 5: game system


   Optional Rules
Spending Luck
After the player has made a skill roll (using a skill or
characteristic), Luck points may be spent to alter the result. The
player can use Luck points to alter a roll on a 1-for-1 basis. The
points spent are deducted from the investigator’s Luck score,
which will reduce the chance of passing a future Luck roll.
    Luck points may not be spent on Luck rolls, damage rolls,
Sanity rolls, or rolls to determine the amount of Sanity points
lost. A player may spend any amount of Luck points (up to
their current Luck value) on a roll. A player may only spend
Luck to alter one of their own dice rolls.
    When a skill roll is failed, the player has the option to
push the roll OR spend luck; Luck points may not be spent to
alter the result of a pushed roll.
    Criticals, fumbles, and firearm malfunctions always apply,
and cannot be bought off with Luck points.
    Also, no skill improvement check is earned if Luck points
were used to alter the dice roll.
    In play, an investigator’s Luck will fall through spends and
rise through recovery (see below).


Recovering Luck points
After each session of play, each player may make an
improvement check for their Luck. This is rolled in the same
way as for skill improvement. The player rolls 1D100 and
if the roll is above their present Luck score they add 1D10
points to their Luck score. If the roll is equal to or less than
the investigator’s present Luck score, no points are recovered.
    Note: An investigator’s Luck score will vary throughout
play, but may not exceed 99. The Luck starting value is not
used again in the game. There are no resets and the starting
value can be exceeded in play.



    The Keeper rolls an Extreme success for a Dark
    Young to trample Harvey into the dust. Harvey fails
    to dodge with a roll of 35, far above his Dodge skill
    of 27. This is life or death, and so Harvey’s player
    spends 30 of Harvey’s Luck points to convert the
    Dodge roll to an Extreme success. Harvey avoids
    death by a hairsbreadth!
        At the start of the following session (Harvey
    having somehow survived to fight another day),
    Harvey’s player makes a check for Luck recovery.
    Harvey’s Luck is now down to 15 points. The
    player rolls 37 and so adds 1D10 to Harvey’s Luck,
    increasing it to 21. Let’s hope Harvey doesn’t have to
    rely on his luck today.

      Improvised Weapons
      All sorts of items can be used to inflict physical harm: snooker
      cues, scissors, bottles, rocks and so on. Even a gramophone
      player could be smashed over the back of someone’s head for
      a nasty injury.
           The Keeper should determine the damage done by an
      improvised weapon by using a comparable item on Table
      XVII: Weapons (pages 401-405). If the improvised weapon
      is similar to a basic melee weapon, such as a knife or club, it
      may be used with the Fighting (Brawl) skill. If it is not, the
      Keeper should decide on which Fighting skill specialization
      is appropriate.
           Everyone is strongly encouraged to make use of the
      environment in a fight. Doing so will add a unique feel to                Frying pans make excellent improvised weapons.
      each fight and will make the setting come to life. If every
      fight uses the same old punches, kicks, and regular weapons,       swords may be to hand in a mansion. Computer monitors,
      they may feel repetitive. Picture the scene and grab what          power cables, and scissors could all be deadly in an office. As
      comes to mind. If the fight takes place in a bar, you should       Keeper, encourage your players to improvise and don’t block
      be swinging pool cues, throwing bottles, and hitting each          their ideas without good reason. If you want to introduce the
      other with barstools. Fire pokers, hat stands, and ornamental      hand of fate to determine whether that handy improvised
                                                                         weapon is present, call for a Luck roll.

                                          chapter 6: combat


Fighting Maneuvers
If a player describes a goal other than simply inflicting
physical harm, such as disarming their opponent or knocking
them over, then it can be resolved with a maneuver. This
may include pushing someone out of the way, throwing the
opponent to the floor, restraining someone in a headlock,
disarming an opponent, and so on.

Step 1: Compare Builds
Clearly skill is an important factor in performing a successful
fighting maneuver, but size and strength also have an effect.
A small, skillful fighter might unbalance or disarm a larger
opponent, but there are limits. No human is ever going to
be able to wrestle a huge monster such as a Dark Young
of Shub-Niggurath to the ground, whatever their skill.
Conveniently, Size and Strength are already factored into
each character’s Build, and thus by comparing these we may
gauge the relative might of the opponents.
    To determine the relative capability of the two opponents
and whether the character performing the maneuver
takes penalty dice, simply compare the Build of the two
combatants. If the character performing the maneuver has
a smaller Build than their opponent then he or she takes a
                                                                                 Old Man Birch has a good left hook.


    Harvey: DEX 55
              Fighting (Brawl) skill: 25 (Regular) / 12 (Hard) / 5 (Extreme)
              Dodge skill: 27 (Regular) / 13 (Hard) / 5 (Extreme)
    Old Man Birch: DEX 60
              Fighting (Brawl) skill: 40 (Regular) / 20 (Hard) / 8 (Extreme)
              Dodge skill: 30 (Regular) / 15 (Hard) / 6 (Extreme)
    The first round:
        Things go badly for Harvey when he fails to sneak around the house, leading to an encounter with Old Man Birch,
    who tries to punch the intruder.
        Old Man Birch has the higher DEX and therefore goes first.
    Old Man Birch’s Action:
        The Keeper picks up the dice and states that Old Man Birch is swinging a fist at Harvey. Harvey’s reaction is to duck.
    The Keeper will therefore roll against Old Man Birch’s Fighting (Brawl) skill and will be opposed by Harvey’s Dodge skill.
        The Keeper rolls 04 for Old Man Birch’s Fighting (Brawl) skill—an Extreme success.
        Harvey requires an Extreme level of success to avoid the blow. Harvey’s player rolls 25 for Harvey’s Dodge skill—a
    Regular success. Not good enough.
        Old Man Birch deals 1D3 damage for an unarmed attack, to which is added his 1D4 damage bonus. As it was an
    Extreme success, the maximum result is used in place of rolling dice; Harvey takes the full 7 points of damage (3 + 4 = 7).
        The Keeper tells how Old Man Birch catches Harvey with a left hook before he has a chance to flee. This is followed
    up with a couple of solid jabs as Harvey reels back, blood running down his face. A smile spreads over Old Man Birch’s
    face as he gets a taste of victory.
    Harvey’s action:
        Harvey is cornered and lashes out at his foe. Old Man Birch attempts to side-step and clout Harvey again. His
    intentions include damage, so this means he is fighting back.
        Harvey’s player rolls 25 for Harvey’s Fighting (Brawl) skill—a Regular success.
        The Keeper rolls 23 for Old Man Birch’s Fighting (Brawl) skill—a Regular success.
        When the target is fighting back, the attacker wins in the case of a tie. Harvey rolls 1D3 for 2 points of damage.
        That’s the end of round one.
